heading: Project Overview
content: Novus Agenti (Omni Claw) is a localized, hyper-customized mobile operating environment that treats individual apps, terminal endpoints, and cloud runtimes as modular subroutines. By separating heavy math (model inference) from the UI layer, the phone acts as an iron-clad local orchestrator for a multi-agent neural mesh rather than a simple app container. Core stack: a Kotlin UI app (lightweight frontend orchestrator) paired with a detached native C++ background daemon (ort_server) running a 6.2GB Gemma-4 12B QAT model on the phone's Hexagon NPU, communicating over a local loopback WebSocket/HTTP connection at 127.0.0.1:8080. Qualcomm QNN SDK static graph partitioning splits the workload: about 5.5GB to the Hexagon NPU, about 0.6GB to the Adreno GPU for custom layers, about 0.1GB to the CPU for housekeeping. This is a Gemini brainstorming session, not verified working code -- treat all mechanisms as design intent.

heading: Core Architecture -- The Watchdog Recovery Bridge
content: An isolated Watchdog Application layer runs alongside the primary Kotlin UI without the core model weights ever being aware of it, to keep the system bulletproof during heavy runtime operations. The Sandbox Blindspot: the 6.2GB model runs fully containerized inside the C++ daemon context, communicating purely via JSON over loopback, with zero awareness of Android UI lifecycle or Kotlin process constraints. The Skeleton Crew Failback: the Watchdog is a featherweight foreground service (~15MB) monitoring the process lifecycle of NpuClient.kt; if a memory spike or un-fused tensor forces a foreground UI crash, the Watchdog intercepts the teardown instantly. Invisible Hot-Reboot: the C++ engine is anchored to the root init tree at a privileged -950 oom_score_adj score, making it unaffected by a foreground UI crash; the Watchdog reads the engine's persistent log cache, captures the exact state sequence, and silently hot-reboots the Kotlin Orchestrator container. State Preservation via WebSockets: a local loopback WebSocket connection continually pushes JSON state frames to a micro-database in local storage, so conversation history, active variables, and pipeline paths recover in milliseconds without reloading the 6.2GB tensor graph.

heading: Agentic Capabilities Map
content: Structured inventory of distinct agentic capabilities described across the session. Global voice dictation: triggered by the standard floating microphone tile tapped anywhere; raw speech goes through a local low-latency cleanup pass (strips filler/stutter/typos) then Android Accessibility Service types pristine text directly into the active cursor field, with no reasoning or tool calls. Meta-prompt compilation (verified): triggered by the internal mic icon inside the AI Chat Floating Box; speech goes through Silero VAD isolation, local loopback stream to the C++ engine, which pulls Obsidian/Markor skill blocks and compiles a structured meta-prompt that streams back into the AI Chat Box as editable text for human review before send -- never auto-dispatched. Local file operations: model emits tool-call JSON routed via Android IPC Intents to a Tasker Relay (intent://com.tasker.TRIGGER) for reading/writing Markdown in Markor or Obsidian vaults, scanning directory trees, and file sorting. System actions: model emits tool-call JSON handled via Android Accessibility Service frameworks to launch cameras, initiate downloads, or pull git repos, targeting about 150MB app overhead. Local shell execution: routed through a Termux GLIBC-patched environment via an authenticated JSON-RPC loopback on port 8022, for bash shortcuts, Python data transformers, patchelf-glibc binary patching, and GitHub/Hugging Face repo interaction. Cloud compute offload: via Google Colab CLI with --auth adc Application Default Credentials, for headless remote T4 GPU provisioning (colab new --gpu T4) and script transmission (colab exec). Agnostic cloud API routing: an OpenAI-compatible translation layer over public tunnels scales out to OpenRouter or fires scripts to remote worker nodes. Fast-pass reasoning: a C++ token parser intercepts <think>/</think> tags from any SSE token stream (e.g. DeepSeek-R1) and emits status frames instead of raw chain-of-thought dump. Continuous screenshot parsing: an automatic background AOSP screen capture loop maps multi-app pipeline navigation and flashes highlighted guides. Contextual point-and-shoot vision: triggered by the camera icon in the AI Chat Floating Box for single-frame capture plus verbal prompt, giving step-by-step troubleshooting directions for dense UI. Local storage directory sniffer: an automatic file observer on /sdcard/Pictures/Screenshots/ pulls raw image bytes into model context instantly on a standard screenshot gesture. Session/share export: the share icon in the AI Chat Floating Box bundles code/output and exports to Drive or Docs. Multi-session management: carbon tabs in the AI Chat Floating Box hot-swap between separate conversation sessions or engineering branches. Crash recovery: the Watchdog service plus WebSocket state frames hot-reboot the Kotlin UI without reloading model weights. Multi-window/multi-device orchestration: split-screen or TigerVNC cast (e.g. to a Tab S9 FE at port 5901) drives a 3-panel layout mimicking a Cloud Shell Editor for parallel cloud/vision plus local shell plus chat orchestration across devices.

heading: The Dual-Tile Floating UI
content: Corrected mid-session by the user to have strictly separated scopes preventing accidental command dispatch. Tile A, the Global Microphone Tile: system-wide scope across any active Android input field, behaving as a Gboard replacement -- raw voice goes through a local cleanup pass removing filler words/stutters/typos, then Accessibility Service types directly into the active cursor field, with no reasoning, no meta-prompting, and no tool calls. Tile B, the AI Chat Floating Box, an expandable sandbox with: a Plus-sign Upload Tray to attach local files/schemas/images to context; a Camera Icon for point-and-shoot screen capture vision troubleshooting; an internal Sandbox Microphone Icon dedicated to agentic-command dictation, separate from Tile A; a Pause Button to freeze token streaming or halt cloud execution mid-flight; Carbon Tabs to organize/hot-swap separate conversation sessions or engineering branches; and a Share Icon to bundle code/logs/output and export to Drive or Docs. The Meta-Prompt Verification Loop is the human-in-the-loop safety mechanism: tap the internal mic in the AI Chat Box and speak a rough high-level intent; Silero VAD (sub-5MB ONNX, 30ms evaluation loop) isolates the speech; the raw text/audio routes over local loopback to the C++ engine; the engine synthesizes the request against skill blocks/API schemas/context pulled from local Obsidian/Markor directories (this is the slot the OpenWiki+OB1+reasoning-bank memory system is meant to fill); the compiled meta-prompt streams back into the AI Chat Box as editable text, not auto-executed; the human reviews, edits, clips parameters, or flags corrections; manual send dispatches to the target local or cloud execution endpoint.

heading: Multi-Window Neural Mesh Layout
content: Whether in split-screen on a tablet or cast via TigerVNC to a secondary display such as a Samsung Tab S9 FE at port 5901, the layout mimics a Cloud Shell Editor environment with three panels. Panel 1, Cloud & Vision Space, runs on Android WebView or a local browser interface, housing live browser instances, remote Colab developer loops, cloud buckets, and visualization graphs. Panel 2, Local Command Cockpit, runs on a patched Termux GLIBC terminal multiplexer, running terminal CLIs side-by-side such as the claude-code CLI on a Pro subscription alongside a DeepSeek-R1 logic solver via an ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL override. Panel 3, the Omni-Claw Chat Panel, runs the Kotlin frontend orchestrator client as the central conductor -- STT input, thinking-state display, execution logs, and file trays.

heading: Local vs. Cloud Agentic Tool Execution Map
content: The Local Loop routes Kotlin App calls through a Tasker Relay to Local Files/Obsidian. The Cloud Loop routes Termux Shell calls through the Colab CLI (with ADC auth) to remote compilation/git. Three execution zones: Local Device Loop uses Android IPC Intents and a Tasker Relay, firing payloads to intent://com.tasker.TRIGGER for reading/writing Markor/Obsidian, file sorting, and native system status. Local Shell Loop uses a Termux GLIBC-patched environment via an authenticated JSON-RPC loopback on port 8022, for terminal workspace navigation, bash shortcuts, patchelf-glibc, and git hooks. Cloud Engine Loop uses the Google Colab CLI with --auth adc for headless T4 GPU provisioning (colab new --gpu T4), Python script transmission (colab exec), and Qualcomm AI Engine SDK model compilation. Workload distribution rationale: floating windows and overlays go to Kotlin native code because Android WindowManager handles smooth persistent rendering over other apps; screen vision ingestion goes to Kotlin native code because AOSP capture loops pull frame bytes into memory faster than any script; bash/git/CLI compiling goes to Termux as a headless socket because it's a native Linux ecosystem compiled for Android without OS restrictions; system automation and macros go to Tasker/MacroDroid intents because they rapidly alter device settings and automate third-party UI steps via clean intent pipelines.

heading: Triple-Mode Screen Vision Framework
content: Three separate computer vision ingestion modes. Mode 1, Continuous Screenshot Parsing: a background AOSP screen capture loop ingests frames over time, tuned for structural navigation mapping across multiple interconnected apps such as walking a user through an Obsidian to Markor to Drive to GitHub pipeline, cross-referencing the current step against the visual UI and flashing highlighted guides. Mode 2, Contextual Point-and-Shoot: triggered only by the Camera Icon in the AI Chat Floating Box, a single-frame capture plus verbal prompt for troubleshooting dense cloud console/IAM-style dashboards; it detects UI/layout drift over time such as a dated post-update navigation change and reroutes accordingly. Mode 3, Local Storage Directory Sniffer: a fallback loop for when continuous streaming throttles performance, watching /sdcard/Pictures/Screenshots/; the moment a hardware-button screenshot lands, the app pulls the raw image bytes straight into the model's visual context window with zero manual upload lag.

heading: Fast-Pass Reasoning -- Thinking Token Suppression
content: To avoid the interface feeling slow from reading raw chain-of-thought text before an answer prints, the C++ engine intercepts reasoning-model tag streams at the parser layer. Because the C++ daemon manages the SSE token stream over 127.0.0.1:8080, it can block raw reasoning text at the source: the moment the opening <think> tag is seen, the daemon drops the raw text and emits a single lightweight JSON frame {"status": "thinking"}; the Kotlin client shows a clean animated 'Thinking...' indicator; the moment the closing </think> tag appears, the state flips to {"status": "generating"} or {"status": "output"} and finalized answer tokens stream through normally.

heading: Corrections Made Mid-Session -- Locked-In Design Decisions
content: The user explicitly corrected two things during this session, which should be preserved as locked-in decisions rather than one of several floated ideas. First, tile separation is strict: the global microphone tile must behave exactly like a Gboard-style dictation replacement with clean typo/filler correction and direct text injection, with no meta-prompting and no reasoning; all meta-prompt compilation happens only via the internal mic inside the AI Chat Floating Box specifically so the user can verify and edit before anything is dispatched. Second, thinking tokens should be suppressed rather than displayed verbatim -- a simple Claude-style 'Thinking...' indicator is preferred over dumping raw chain-of-thought text, since reading through it slows the user down without adding value if final answer quality doesn't depend on seeing it.

heading: Open Questions / Not Yet Resolved
content: Several items were raised in the session but not locked down as final decisions. Whether local tool use/inference should route entirely through Tasker/Termux, or whether Android runtimes/Kotlin build code can streamline or replace parts of that split -- the session leans toward the hybrid Kotlin-broker plus Termux-headless-daemon plus Tasker-macro-glue split, but this was presented as a recommended answer, not something independently confirmed elsewhere by the user. The exact desktop/laptop version of the app is described only as 'a future version,' not scoped in this session. P2P capabilities between the user's Motorola phone and other AI Dev SoCs/SoMs are mentioned as a target capability but not architected in this session.

heading: Relationship to AESOP
content: AESOP is confirmed by the user as the Termux-nerfed subset of this Omni Claw vision -- same voice-first / meta-prompt-compilation / device-profile concept, but running inside Termux plus PRoot-Ubuntu with cloud Claude API delegation instead of a native Kotlin app with local Gemma-4 NPU inference, with no Watchdog, no floating tiles, no screen vision, and no Tasker/Colab tool-execution split. See the separate aesop-full-reference doc for the detailed side-by-side comparison table.

heading: Relationship to the OpenWiki + OB1 + reasoning-bank memory system
content: The Meta-Prompt Verification Loop's 'Local Knowledge Synthesis' step -- the C++ engine pulling skill blocks, API schemas, and historical code contexts directly out of local Obsidian or Markor directories before compiling a meta-prompt -- is the exact function the separate wiki/memory synthesis project is meant to formalize and back with real infrastructure: OB1's thoughts table plus remote MCP as the actual store, OpenWiki's SKILL.md convention as the maintenance layer, and reasoning-bank's success/failure memory-item pattern as the retrieval strategy, replacing ad hoc 'read some Obsidian files' behavior with a structured, queryable memory backend.
